video_title: The Dollar System Is Losing Trust — Gold’s Monetary Reset Has Begun
transcript: The problem with gold is, as you know, it's not about gold actually. Cuz gold doesn't change at all. It's about everything else. We are in a normal gold cycle or if this is a a monetary cycle where we're actually at the beginning or right in the middle of a monetary revaluation of gold. It's quite plausible that that central banks sooner or later will also buy small strategic Bitcoin positions alongside gold. The industry is basically um should practice what what what they preach. They produce um this excellent hedge against that inflation, but they sell like everything. >> Greetings and welcome to our Wealthion Show. And they said, "Yeah, it's a good movie." Um especially the first one, second one not so much, third one uh um but I think it's you know, if if you really want to fix the present or or especially the future, we have to go back into history and I think that um um if you want to understand all the the issues and the problems and the conflicts that we have nowadays, obviously you have to understand our monetary history and that's what it's all about. So, I would say that the the you know, if we zoom out, I think what we try to to analyze this year is really the question if we are in a normal gold cycle or if this is a a monetary cycle where we're actually at the beginning or right in the middle of a monetary revaluation of gold. >> Excellent. And uh one of the first uh ideas that you bring forth and I it's impossible to address all of all all of the report, but I'm going to try to dance around to uh things that I thought really s- you know, stuck out. You have this concept that the Pax Americana is coming to a close. So, I know you could talk about this for half an hour, but give me a precis of your thought process there. >> Yeah, well, it's I think it's uh as Hemingway uh would have said, gradually then suddenly. I I think the topic of of de-dollarization is something that we're writing about for for more than a decade and now it's really becoming it's becoming mainstream. Um we've seen it with the recent uh visit of Donald Trump in in China, for example. It seems that um uh it seemed actually that that that the Chinese were extremely confident and and I think in in all sorts of of areas, be it uh technology, the the economy, um science, um it seems that China is at least um on a how do say, on a level playing field these days and and and really um, has picked up lots of momentum. And I think this um, you know, it's what when I called the report In Gold We Trust back then, um, in 2007 I was still a uh uh uh young analyst in a in a in a Viennese bank uh full hair um uh you know, 2007 that's that's really long time ago that back then, you know, as we write in the report, the US had, I think, six bill um six trillion in government debt. Um, we haven't even seen the the the iPhone, Netflix was still mailing DVDs. So, that's a long time ago, but when when we called the report In Gold We Trust, I wasn't really aware of um the importance of the topic of trust and and and 2019 we we wrote a report called Gold in the Age of um the um erosion of trust. And I think that's what that's what we're seeing. It's it's only not only this loss of trust in in science, uh it's obviously in politics, it's in institutions um OPEC, for example, United Nations uh NATO. Um, it's a loss of trust in >> [snorts] >> America as the leader of the free world. Um, with Donald Trump it's pretty obvious that that uh he wants to focus on um the the Monroe or the Don Road doctrine. Um, it's a loss of trust when it comes to central bankers and the independence and also the competence of central bankers. Um, so I think it's it's really um the fact that the price of gold is is rising that dramatically. And I mean, last year um up 65% in dollar terms. I mean, that was a huge move. But, it also, if you if you change the perspective, it means that's an enormous loss of purchasing power of the dollar measured in gold. And this is really a trend that we're seeing for quite a while, and now it seems suddenly it's it's it's it's picking up um more momentum. So, you know, from my point of view, it's everybody is obviously a little bit nervous and anxious regarding the current um uh the current uh price action in gold. We really got to zoom out and and and and and try to to to discuss the question if this is a monetary cycle or not. Because in monetary terms, if you compare gold, for example, if you do the shadow gold price, if you do um gold as a percentage of of M0 or of M2, if you compare it to to the evaluation in equity markets, gold is actually still I would say at pretty reasonable prices. So, I think people, you know, shouldn't just focus on the nominal price, but also have, you know, um have a look a closer look at relative charts. That's That's what we do quite a lot. And then analyze everything from a monetary point of view. And I think, you know, being European, my grandparents lived through, I think, four or five different fiat currencies. Um, with every currency reform, they basically lost everything. So, for them, having gold as a as a monetary insurance policy was just normal. While if you're American or or or British, obviously, you know, there's there's um gold isn't in the in the, let's say, monetary DNA. Um, I think Americans are especially um influenced by the Great Depression and the fear of deflation. But from my point of view, uh we shouldn't fear deflation. Can you just give me a I don't know, a few sentences on where that places us in the ultimate >> Sure. So, So, when I was still in the bank, my boss told me, "You have to do the CFA." And I said, "Well, I don't know. Um uh probably wasn't smart enough, and I and I had a a a new girlfriend, now now my wife, actually. And I said, "I don't have the time, so I I'll do the CMT, the Chartered Market Technician." >> Mhm. >> Um which is less work, but I really enjoyed it. Um it's it's lots of, you know, reading books about um technical analysis, you know, the market structure, sentiment, and that has heavily influenced my my view on markets, you know, intermarket analysis, sentiment analysis, positioning, and so on. And I think the foundation of technical analysis is is basically by by by by the Dow theory and and Charles Dow, who founded the Wall Street Journal and who calculated the Dow Jones Industrials Index first, he said that every big secular trend has three different stages. First one is the accumulation phase when only die-hard contrarians, they you know, they buy against extremely negative sentiment. So, if you're at the cocktail party and say, "I'm buying gold," people will ridicule you. You're like the dumbest idiot. Um So, this is the first stage when, you know, contrarians buy against against an extremely negative market sentiment. But, this is also the moment when when the internals and the fundamentals actually start to to to become more positive. Then, the second phase is the so-called public participation phase. And I think we're somewhere like in the middle or I know you like baseball, so I think probably like in the sixth sixth inning um of this trend. So, gold isn't a contrarian asset anymore, yeah? I mean, that's pretty obvious. Um but this public participation phase is by far the longest one, and this is the one the phase where the media picks up um the topic, where analysts start becoming a little bit more positive. Just compared to last year, last year when we published the report, gold was significantly lower, and basically every analyst out there um from all the major banks was negative on gold forecasting you know sideways trend and then a big bear market until the end of the decade. Now with rising prices they also erased their price targets and now they're slightly more positive.
